Q1: I tried to organize my study routine around my work schedule and other responsibilities, which meant I usually studied in chunks whenever I could find time. I'm a read-write learner, so I focused heavily on reading materials and writing out notes by hand. I aimed for about 13 hours a week, but honestly, those hours were scattered throughout the week rather than in one solid block. I kept a small planner where I'd write down what I needed to cover, and I'd check things off as I went.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I could stick to a more regular schedule, but other times deadlines or personal stuff would throw everything off. I'm 25, so I have responsibilities outside of school that younger students might not have, and that meant I had to be flexible. When assignments were due, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks I might spread things out more evenly.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check assignment deadlines or download reading materials. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a new announcement or a discussion post, and that would prompt me to log in. I wasn't someone who just browsed around the LMS for fun—I went in with a specific purpose, got what I needed, and then worked offline. Checking grades after submitting work also got me to log in.
Q4: There were periods where logging in became more routine, especially at the start of the semester when I was trying to get organized. I'd make it a habit to check in every few days to see if anything new was posted. But as the course went on and I figured out where everything was, I logged in less frequently because I'd already downloaded what I needed. The routine kind of faded once I had my materials sorted.
Q5: The reading materials and written lecture notes were the most useful for me because that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs, print them out, and annotate them in my own way. The discussion boards were also helpful when I participated because hearing other students' perspectives helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. I found that anything I could take offline and work through at my own pace was valuable.
Q6: I honestly didn't watch many of the videos—my video watch time was pretty low because I retain information better through reading than watching. I'd sometimes start a video but then feel like I could get the same information faster by reading the transcript or notes. The videos just didn't match my learning style, so I'd skim through them or skip them entirely if there were written alternatives available.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I needed to know for assignments or if it helped me understand a difficult concept. I'm practical about my studying because I don't have unlimited time. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my grade, I was less motivated to engage with it. I appreciated activities that had clear purposes and outcomes.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—I treated them as 'nice to have' rather than essential. With required activities, I'd make sure to complete them no matter what, even if I was tired or busy. But optional ones? I'd only do them if I had extra time or if I was genuinely interested. Given everything on my plate, I had to prioritize, and optional stuff usually fell to the bottom of the list.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and notes, print them out, and study from paper. I also used my own notebooks extensively—I'd rewrite key concepts and create summaries by hand because that helps me remember things. I had some external textbooks and resources I'd found online that supplemented the course materials. The LMS was really just my starting point for gathering materials.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with the course content but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours reading through printed materials at the library or at home, writing out practice answers, and reviewing my handwritten notes. None of that shows up anywhere in the system. My best studying happened away from the computer, with a pen in my hand and papers spread out in front of me.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything I needed for a module or unit, and then not touch the LMS again until the next batch of materials was posted. I'd access a PDF once, save it to my computer, print it, and then study from the printed version for days or even weeks. One login might represent hours of subsequent offline studying.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement became harder, especially around midterms when I had multiple things due at once. I also went through a period where my anxiety spiked and I felt overwhelmed by how much I had to do. The CBT sessions I attended helped me manage that anxiety, but during those rough patches, I'd avoid logging in because even seeing the LMS stressed me out. I pushed through, but it wasn't easy.
Q13: Workload and competing responsibilities were constant factors for me. There were weeks when work demanded more of my time, and I'd have to cut back on how much I engaged with the LMS. Fatigue was real too—by the time I got home some nights, I just couldn't focus on a screen anymore. Confusion with certain topics also slowed me down because I'd spend more time trying to understand the basics rather than moving forward.
Q14: Yeah, there were times when I'd open materials but my mind was elsewhere. I'd scroll through readings without really absorbing anything because I was tired or distracted. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open while dealing with other things, so technically I was 'accessing' materials but not really learning. Those sessions felt unproductive, and I'd have to go back and redo the work later when I could actually focus.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially exam preparation. I knew studying for exams was crucial, but I kept putting it off because assignments felt more urgent—they had specific deadlines. I'd tell myself I'd study for the exam after finishing the next assignment, but then another assignment would come up. My exam score really suffered because of this pattern, even though my assignment completion was high.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. I'd structure my entire week around what was due and when. As a deadline approached, my LMS activity would spike because I'd be checking requirements, submitting work, and making sure I hadn't missed anything. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less with the LMS. They kept me accountable, even if they also stressed me out sometimes.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though I didn't always get it as quickly as I would have liked. When I received good feedback, it motivated me to keep going and put effort into the next assignment. When feedback pointed out mistakes, I'd try to learn from it, though sometimes it was discouraging. I wished there was more timely feedback so I could adjust my approach before the next deadline.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation quite a bit. Having 10 online courses meant I had to juggle a lot, and this particular course's structure sometimes got lost in the shuffle. When modules were clearly organized with explicit deadlines and requirements, I could plan better. But when things were vague or spread across different sections of the LMS, I'd get confused and miss things.
Q19: Clear organization made engagement easier—when I could see exactly what was expected and when. What made it harder was when important information was buried in multiple places or when instructions were unclear. Also, the heavy reliance on video content was tough for me as a read-write learner. I would have appreciated more written alternatives to the video lectures. The discussion requirements were manageable and actually helped me engage more.
Q20: I think students might show high LMS activity but submit little work if they're struggling to understand the material. They might be searching through resources repeatedly, trying to figure things out, but never feeling confident enough to submit. Or they could be dealing with anxiety or perfectionism—wanting everything to be perfect before turning it in. Life circumstances might also mean they're logging in with good intentions but can't follow through.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My attendance was really low and my login frequency wasn't high, but I completed almost all my assignments. I did a lot of work offline that wouldn't show up in the LMS data. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't engaged, but I was putting in solid hours—just not in ways the system could track. The exam score hurt me, but my assignment work was consistent.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone submitted work and how often they logged in, which gives a basic picture of activity patterns. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether someone was actually learning or just clicking through. They can't capture offline studying, the quality of someone's understanding, or the personal circumstances affecting their performance. Logs show behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material and caring about my own learning, not just going through the motions. It means putting in effort even when things are hard and seeking help when I'm stuck. For me, engagement also means connecting what I'm learning to my own goals and life. It's an internal commitment that doesn't always show up in external metrics like login counts.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I struggled with test anxiety, which is why my exam score was so much lower than my assignment scores. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying from printed materials or handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture the personal growth I experienced through the CBT sessions or how I managed to stay resilient despite wanting to give up sometimes. My journey through this course was more complex than any data could capture.
